exemplar.title: How effective have government policies been in addressing rental housing affordability and availability in London from 2020 to 2024?

detailedExemplarData.title: How effective have government policies been in addressing rental housing affordability and availability in London from 2020 to 2024?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Topic and purpose are communicated accurately and effectively throughout.
Research question is clearly stated, focused and consistently connected to the analysis.
Section headings and content remain tightly aligned to the central focus.
weaknesses: Methodology could justify the chosen 2020â2024 period more explicitly.
Range of sources is appropriate but could be expanded with primary data.
Minor digressions into broad macro context slightly dilute focus.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation excels at defining its scopeâgovernment rental policies in London, 2020â2024âand sustaining that focus. The research question is crystal-clear, evaluative and effectively framed. While the methodology is sound and justified, the rationale for omitting primary data and the period choice could be more explicitly defended. Overall, this aligns with the 5â6 band; given its sustained clarity and efficacy, a topâband mark of 6 is warranted.

strengths: Accurate use of core economic terminology (consumer surplus, PED, YED) in most contexts.
Source material is generally relevant, drawing on reputable official data and audit reports.
Demonstrates clear and coherent knowledge of Londonâs rental market and policy history.
weaknesses: Inconsistent notation (e.g., missing negative sign for PED) and occasional misstatements in surplus calculations.
Application of some data is mechanical; stylised diagrams sometimes replace actual figures without annotation.
Key formulas and source citations (e.g., Drupal reference, figure origins) need fuller documentation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Terminology and conceptual knowledge are mostly accurate, and sources are appropriately chosen, yet there are recurring small inaccuracies and gaps in citation/detail. The work consistently fulfils the descriptors of the 3â4 band but does not yet demonstrate the precision for a 5â6. Therefore, a midâband score of 3 reflects its clear but imperfect mastery.

strengths: Research draws on multiple official data sets and triangulates sources to address the question.
Analysis employs surplus, elasticity and policyâmatrix tools with generally wellâsupported conclusions.
Evaluation constructs a reasoned argument comparing two programmes and proposes alternatives.
weaknesses: All evidence is secondary; no primary interviews or surveys are included to deepen insight.
Depth of analysis is limited by simplified assumptions (e.g., geometric surplus approximations).
Critical evaluation lacks quantitative support for some causal claims (e.g., inflationâconstruction costs).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation shows clear analysis with mostly supported conclusions and a reasoned comparative evaluation of policies. Research is appropriate and relevant, but the reliance on secondary estimates and limited empirical testing prevents a higher band. This performance aligns with the 4â6 band; a mark of 5 reflects its solid but not outstanding critical thinking.

strengths: Overall structure follows EE conventions with logical sequencing and clear section headings.
Elements such as title page, word count and bibliography are correctly presented.
weaknesses: Page numbering is missing and inâtext citations are inconsistently formatted.
Some tables overflow margins and diagrams lack proper labels and units.
Table of contents omits page references, reducing navigational clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reportâs structure is generally appropriate, but formatting issuesâmissing page numbers, inconsistent citations, poorly formatted tables/figuresâindicate that only some layout conventions are met. This corresponds to the 1â2 band; a score of 2 accurately reflects the presentation quality.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly worded as âHow effective have government policies been in addressing rental housing affordability and availability in London from 2020 to 2024?â  It is focused, contains clear evaluative language (effectiveness), specifies policies, a city, a market segment and a time horizon.  Nearly every analytical section (surplus, PED/YED, policy evaluation) refers back to affordability/availability.  Some digressions occur (e.g., broad macro discussion of inflation) so absolute precision is slightly diluted; hence a solid 5 rather than 6.

The student states the overall topic (government intervention in Londonâs rental housing) explicitly in the title and reiterates it clearly in the introduction.  The purposeâassessing effectiveness of two Affordable Homes Programmes on affordability and availability between 2020-24âis sharply delimited by place, policy instruments and time frame.  Throughout the essay, section headings and analysis remain tightly aligned to this focus, so readers can immediately see how each part links to the central purpose.  This level of clarity and sustained relevance matches the descriptors for the top band.

Citations come predominately from ONS, National Audit Office, Greater London Authority and reputable news outlets, all directly pertinent to Londonâs rental sector and the chosen programmes.  However, several key numerical inputs are acknowledged as âapproximatedâ, and some diagrams use stylised rather than actual data.  While mostly relevant, the application is occasionally mechanical (e.g., simple cost comparisons across cities without adjusting for house type).  This aligns with âmostly relevant and appropriateâ, justifying a mid-band score of 3.

The essay follows EE conventions: title page, abstract-style introduction, table of contents, sequential sections (introduction, methodology, analysis, evaluation, limitations, conclusion, bibliography, appendix).  Transitions are logical and internal headings guide the reader.  Minor structural lapses (some duplicated numbering, very long sections without sub-sub-headings) prevent the highest mark but overall structure is clearly appropriate, warranting 3.

Formatting elementsâtitle, word count (3844), and bibliographyâare present, and diagrams/tables are neatly inserted.  However, there is no clear evidence of page numbering in the transcript, several tables overflow margins, and in-text citations are inconsistently formatted (mix of footnote-style and inline).  These shortcomings indicate that âsome layout considerations are missing or incorrectâ, so 2 is appropriate.

The student structures a comparative discussion of two policies, noting successes, shortfalls, and external shocks.  Evaluation criteria (extent to which objectives were met, cost effectiveness) are explicit, and alternative measures (public-private partnerships) are proposed.  Still, the critique remains largely qualitative and would benefit from deeper consideration of opportunity costs or distributional outcomes.  Consequently, the work fits the âreasoned argument with partially critical evaluationâ descriptor at a solid 4.

The essay applies surplus, elasticity and supply-shift diagrams to track price and quantity changes, and constructs evaluation matrices against explicit criteria.  These demonstrate clear analysis and generally reasoned conclusions (e.g., AHP 2016-23 partially effective).  However, calculations rely on simplified geometry using assumed axes intercepts; causal links (inflation â material costs â completions) are asserted rather than empirically tested, limiting depth.  Thus analysis is âclear with mostly supported conclusionsâ, placing it at 4.

The essay demonstrates a sound grasp of core micro concepts (surplus, elasticity, market failure) and public-sector evaluation ideas.  Background history of âRight to Buyâ and inflationâs impact shows contextual knowledge.  Nonetheless, some misstatements appearâe.g., PED signs treated as positive, inconsistent surplus calculationsâand the link between macro variables and programme delivery is asserted rather than fully substantiated.  The work therefore reflects âclear though partially effectiveâ knowledge, meriting 3.

The bulk of the research (ONS rental datasets, policy audit documents, inflation figures) is appropriate and clearly linked to the question.  The student triangulates different official sources and time-series data.  Limitations are acknowledged.  Nonetheless, all evidence is secondary and some quantities are estimated, preventing the consistency expected of the top band.  Therefore a strong but not outstanding 5 is justified.

The methodology section lists secondary quantitative data from ONS, income figures, and government audit documents, and justifies their use.  Analytical tools (surplus, elasticities, comparative policy matrices) are outlined.  Evidence of selection is present, but the range is limited to a few public databases and newspaper summaries; no primary data or stakeholder interviews are attempted, and approximations are admitted in the appendix.  These characteristics correspond to âgenerally relevant sources with some informed selectionâ, placing the work firmly in the 3-4 band; the clearer planning merits the upper mark of 4.

Economic terminology (consumer surplus, PED, YED, contractionary monetary policy) is used frequently and generally correctly, demonstrating comfortable command.  Yet there are a few inaccuraciesâe.g., calling elasticities âprice elasticâ without the minus sign, confusing surplus magnitudesâand notation is sometimes inconsistent.  This mixture of largely accurate but imperfect usage matches the description of âmostly accurateâ, so 3 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The choice of 2020â2024 period should be justified explicitly; explain why this span best addresses the research question.
Definitions of consumer and producer surplus are accurate, concise and correctly framed.
When defining surplus concepts, include the corresponding algebraic formulas alongside verbal descriptions to deepen conceptual clarity.
Surplus diagrams are stylised; ensure both axes are labelled and scales correspond to actual data values for rigor.
Table 1 presents surplus values but omits currency symbols and unit labels; add âÂ£â and clear headings to avoid ambiguity.
Claim that one-bedroom demand rose due to student influx lacks supporting demographics; integrate enrolment or migration stats.
Title clearly states the investigation topic and focus on government intervention.
Word count provided and within the 4000-word limit, meeting formatting requirements.
No visible page number on this page; include consistent numbering in the footer for ease of navigation.
Table of contents header is clear and follows expected presentation conventions.
The table of contents lists sections but omits page references; add page numbers next to each entry for reader orientation.
The research question is explicitly stated, focused and aligns well with the investigationâs scope.
The introduction presents broad context before scope; consider stating the precise time frame and policy focus earlier for immediate clarity.
Citing âDrupal, 2024â may not be authoritative; replace with a primary or official source (e.g., ONS report) to strengthen reliability.
Methodology outlines secondary sources but does not justify omitting primary data; consider incorporating stakeholder interviews or surveys.
Figure 4 illustrates monetary policy but lacks source citation; specify the text or database where this diagram is drawn from.
The discussion of higher interest rates is logical but would benefit from quantitative data showing construction-cost changes over time.
The PED formula is captioned as a figure; relabel it as an equation (e.g., âEquation 1â) and place in the methodology section.
The PED table shows values for one- and two-bedroom units but omits the three-bedroom calculation; include it or clarify why it was excluded.
Assertion that demand increases with price contradicts PED theory; examine whether housing behaves as a Giffen or Veblen good in this context.
Figure 6 lacks axis labels and units; add âPrice (Â£)â and âQuantityâ labels with scales to enhance interpretability.
The YED formula is formatted as a figure; reclassify it as âEquation 2â and position within the methodology section for consistency.
Interpretation of YED values omits details on income data source and calculation assumptions; include a footnote explaining this.
Figure 8 cites a textbook source but omits edition and page number; add full reference details for reproducibility.
The effectiveness evaluation table is dense and difficult to parse; consider splitting by criterion or using bullet lists to improve readability.
